## [00:00 - 03:01]

I'm going to invite to the podium somebody who is really a list wizard. I invited this person because I didn't realize this. As I mentioned before, Brian Kurtz and I are a good long-term business partner and some friends. We had a lot of successes together. And Brian said, do you know my friend Michael Fishman is really the world's best list broker? So I kind of just thought about that and I said, well, that's good. And when I started researching some of the packages that were my biggest successes, I mean one of them mailed well over 100 million pieces, he was the guy who was kind of behind the scenes, the list wizard of Oz who was helping my client silently. I didn't even know that he was working with them to find much bigger mailing universes to go to. Because you can have a big hit, but if your universe is only 10 million people, that's all you're going to go to. Mike is really a genius at finding out the much bigger universes that may not look like a great mailing universe because they don't maybe necessarily match up the way traditional list brokers make them match up. But he sees similarities where other people don't. And by that process, he's been able to make many of my mailings. I never knew this about him until Brian told me what a good list broker he is the best. And I would like to invite Michael Fishman up because I'll learn here too. I have no idea how he does this to tell us how he does some of his list magic and make smaller universes into bigger universes. So I'm going to... Michael, do you want to come up and talk for about 30 minutes? Good afternoon. As the one lucky person to get to this mic other than Gary and Pauline so far today, I think I'd be remiss if I didn't in 10 seconds acknowledge on behalf of all of you and I trust this is okay. So it's an unquestionable honor for me to be addressing you and to be speaking with you and even though I may be the only one speaking in the next 20 or 30 minutes, I do feel like it's a conversation of sorts because I'll be doing some talking and I know I have your listening and your attention and I know that you'll walk away with something that you can use.

## [03:01 - 06:03]

I was speaking with Chris Morton, who's a gentleman here from the UK, one of the flyers. And Chris said, well, why would a list broker want to be here to hear about this stuff? Most of my 20 years in this profession, what I've done to figure out how to go to the marketplace with the wonderful work that Gary and other writers create is to figure out once, I mean, Gary's spent now five or six hours telling us how to create a direct mail package, but once it's done in consideration of everything that he's studied and worked and discovered over the years, then what do you do with it? Well, it has to go to the marketplace and we need to figure out who wants this stuff. We need to figure out who wants this stuff. Brian Kurtz has referred to me as a behavioral marketing strategist and that's, I'm okay with that. Now Gary's retiring, but I'm not. ## [06:03 - 09:04]

get some value out of that piece. And then we'll look at the advanced course in lists and see what happens when you take the game to a higher level. And then I'll give you some little potpourri of breakthrough strategies at the end, just some other ways of thinking about lists as well. Gary called this portion of the day something that's too laudatory for me to repeat. So that's why I've entitled these next 20 or 30 minutes what Gary just said. So list 101, the crash course, 15 checkpoints to understanding a list. The first thing we want to know about a list, I mean, when we look at a list of subscribers to a newsletter or a magazine or people who've bought a particular product, there's a great deal of dimension and depth that we can go to to actually understand who's on that list, how it ticks, how it works. The ultimate goal, of course, for everybody, myself and you as my client, the writers in the room, is to mail as much mail as possible and to sell as much product as possible. But we want to understand lists as deeply and profoundly as we can and really ultimately bring a lot of certainty to this process of list selection. And the metaphor I like to use is that ultimately you're really putting puppet strings on the marketplace, the greater the extent to which you can write packages which Gary's been talking about, bring a level of certainty to how you're engaging people and speaking to people, and then bring a tremendous degree of certainty to the way in which we're deciding who to contact, then you're really bringing that much more certainty and assuring yourself of either a good result or the product that's mature and are already validated to be working or getting a good barometer reading on a product launch so that you can trust your results and know if you really have a product or not. So number one, the source of a list, where were the transactions generated for those people to get on the list? Was it a space head? Was it a direct mail piece? Was it an infomercial? If it was on TV, was it 30 minutes, two minutes, or one minute? Was it a very different behavior from people responding to television commercials of different length? So we want to know the source which is really about what was the medium. The offer, and in strict direct mail terms, the offer of course refers to the deal. Was it bill me later? Was it three installments? Was it cash with the order? Was there a credit card option? Was there a premium? Was it an editorial premium or a clock radio or an umbrella? All of these things help you gauge the level of commitment on the part of that person when they engaged in that transaction. The price or the monetary, what did they spend? How much money? Ultimately, in a sense, particularly in a bill me environment, the tougher thing to get from people is not response and copywriters might win at that statement, but in a sense we really need to have a great sense of people's commitment.

## [09:04 - 12:08]

And commitment in a bill me environment means that someone will be willing to pay their bill. So we want to have a sense of what people have spent by looking at price or monetary value in conjunction with offer. We have a sense of what they spent and what was the deal inside of they spent it. Mailing cycles, we want to have a sense of how often the company on which that person resides mails. If it's the Wisconsin cheesemen and most of their business, probably more than 80-20, is holiday gift business, then of their million and a half transactions a year, maybe 90% are in the months of October and November. If that list works for us and we expect it to contribute to our campaigns in April, that's really not happening because the bulk of that business is in the fall. Some companies mail every single month and put up those transactions each month in what we call a monthly hotline. And those names are available each month. So that's mailing cycles. Mail piece format, which I'll talk about in a few minutes in the advanced course, was in an envelope, either a number 10, 6x9, a jumbo, what a lot of people refer to as component packages with a letter and a lift note, a brochure possibly, an order card, a BRE, some of the components that are in there, versus a book log or a mag log or a postcard. Regardless of the subject matter you're selling, your price point or anything else, the mail piece format and the way in which a list was generated has a lot to do with the behavior of somebody going forward. We'll talk a little bit about that. Mail piece format, very, very critical. The transaction versus update information. If we want to talk about April 05's hotline update, did those transactions actually happen in April or are they several months old? Does the transaction actually hit the newest update? One of the key principles in direct mail is what we call recency, that people that have bought most frequently are the ones that respond the best. Why? Because a buying event took place very, very recently. So someone who bought something last month is more likely to buy than someone who bought three months ago, six months ago, 12 months ago, etc. So that's recency. It's how often someone buys. And we're going to talk about this a little bit more in a few minutes. Frequency is generally regarded as a good thing because the more someone buys, the better of a purchaser they are. If they bought the air and space ingots from the Franklin Mitten, they've bought 82 of them, that's a pretty good direct mail buyer. I actually have a concern about frequent buyers, which we'll talk about in a few minutes. The universe means if we test a one-month hotline or if we test a subscriber from the last six months or if we test a three-month credit card buyer or any of the permutations we can come up with and have reason to segment, how many names are available on the upside?

## [12:08 - 15:12]

What's the upside of that segmentation and is it worth testing into? If we devise a select that we think will have a select or a segment that we think will have value and productivity for what our mail piece looks like and what our goal is, and we test 6,000 and the entire segment is comprised of 12,000 people, we're investing in the mailing of 6,000 names with not a lot of payoff if that works. So we want to make sure we're testing into meaningful audiences and so that's the universe count for the segment that we're looking at. The service bureau is the company that maintains the database or maintains the list that we're looking to order from. We just want to have a sense that there's integrity there, that it's a service bureau that we know, feel comfortable with and that we know that we can trust the fulfillment will be exactly what we specify, that we'll get the right segment. We can count on that and not have a concern about the fact that they're running machines with rubber bands or something like that. Interest and affinity, if we're publishing a book or a newsletter or a magazine and there may be people selling sweaters here. I know the educational programs, I know a lot of the conversation is around publishing by certainly acknowledges a range of things being sold here, but we do want to have a sense that people are interested in what we're selling, that the people on the list have an interest in what we're selling. There are some topics like the ones we've been discussing a lot today that Gary's been sharing with you, particularly health and personal finance, which in a sense are of interest to everybody or at least could be. At least could be. So there's a universal nature to those subjects that enables the most powerful creative pieces in health and personal finance to be sold to people on lists very frequently that have nothing to do with health and personal finance. And we'll talk about that in a little bit. If you're selling something about golf or fly fishing or quilting, the odds are you need to go to somebody who has engaged in a transaction in those hobbies or in those interest areas before. People either fly fish or they don't, and a mail piece won't talk someone in to fly fishing, not in the numbers you'd need to make it work in direct mail. But when you're talking about a product or a value proposition around health or personal finance, you can, what I call trigger, someone to take responsibility or interest in those areas because sooner or later everybody needs to deal with that. So we'll talk a little bit more about that too. But that's how you think about interest and affinity. Demographics just refers to the percentage of men and women, the average age, income, those kinds of things in terms of the composition and complexion of a list. Most of the time you have to have a sense of your market. The best marketers, I think, can put themselves in the shoes of the marketplace even if they themselves would never be a buyer of that product. And the reason, one reason of the many is that most, other than the magazines which do

## [15:12 - 18:14]

a lot of research with which to sell their ads and have a pretty good sense of who their readerships and constituents are, most of the lists that we look at, we need to infer who those people are. I need to infer who's bought from Neiman Marcus versus Finger Hut versus some non-branded book promising health benefits. That's my inference to make because I'm getting very little research at all or I'm not getting research that I can really rely on. So I need to infer who it is I'm going after based on what the product that was that they bought and the mail piece and the kind of copy that they responded to and what they spent. Usage refers to, well, who else has used the list? In a sense, that's an area of proof for the productivity of the list. So when people sell lists, they usually say, well, so-and-so. These following 28 companies have used it successfully. So that helps. But I think in terms of the work I've done on Gary's packages and certainly one of the very great ones for Rodale Press, now called Rodale Inc., the book New Choices in Natural Healing, had we, or really in any situation, had we limited ourselves to following in the footsteps of other mailers that we found using other lists, we would have mailed a small fraction of what we ultimately mailed. So using your intelligence and your knowledge of the marketplace and distinctions of the kind of which, distinctions like what Gary's been presenting to you and using your intelligence as Dick Benson did say, that'll take you far further than just following the competition. You want market leadership, you want category leadership, and following usage patterns won't enable that at all. But you want to look. Response and back end, you want to have a sense of how different kinds of lists respond, how catalog lists respond to publishing offers and vice versa, the relationship between your upfront orders and your payments if you're in a bilmy environment. You want to have a sense of the cost of a list and whether or not the CPMs, the cost per thousands, are a match for your acquisition economics. That's the math and finance of direct mail. And then you just have to have a great sense of your cost per order metrics so that your mail piece and your list costs and ultimately your overall CPM in the mail is workable for the product you're selling in your overall business model. So now that everybody's caught up hopefully, and hopefully that makes sense, and I acknowledge we don't have time for questions, but I'm certainly available to support you if any of that wasn't clear. I'll be here afterwards. The advanced course in lists, and these are distinctions that I've worked on and come up with over the years in working with probably hundreds of products in the book newsletter

## [18:14 - 21:17]

magazine business and things that are for me what enable me to, so to speak, put the puppet strings on the marketplace. Do what I needed to do, take the great creative that people like Gary are writing and bring it to the marketplace with really a great level of certainty. A lot of this is about the offer I'm mailing and a lot of it is also about the list I'm mailing too so you want to look for matches. The first place you want to look for matches is in what I call branding. So as an example, when I talk about branding, the context in which I refer to branding really refers to the extent to which the name of the firm or the positioning on the mailpiece is known to the recipient whether or not that person has ever done business with you or not. So if your prevention or men's health or bottom line or Julian Whitaker, one of my important considerations is, is there any equity, positive or negative, is there resonance, reverence, familiarity with whoever is claiming credibility or whoever is the positioning name on this mailing. So in the health business as an example, there are personalities like Whitaker and Chopra who Gary just mentioned. In the money business there's Louis Ruchizer and Richard Band whose mailpieces we've been looking at. There are organizational and institutional positionings or brandings in the money business like Morningstar or Motley Fool or American Association of Individual Investors in the health business, the Harvard's, the Mayo's. And these are faceless but highly revered institutional or organizational voices. There are commercial brands like Prevention and Men's Health. There are consumer advocates like Consumer Reports or Nutrition Action or Public Citizen and Ralph Nader. So we want to have a sense of which of those are we marketing with and in many cases some of you may be marketing with no brand. In which event you're purely, you're living and breathing and your jet fuel is your copy and your benefits and your value proposition. So we want to look at do we have a brand or any recognition in the marketplace, how would we characterize it or do we have no brand at all. So as an example of the implication around that, we could look at two health newsletters like the Harvard Women's Health Watch which actually could mail a list like Neiman Marcus. Why would that be? You know, those are, the Neiman Marcus buyers are people that appreciate prestigious brands and want to be associated with prestigious names. So even though catalog buyers don't open envelopes very readily and even though we certainly don't know whether the Neiman Marcus buyers have any interest in health, they're getting

an envelope from Harvard and that's kind of an acknowledgement. Someone could think, whoa, Harvard. Harvard wrote to me. One of the things that we help clients to see as we're growing their business is that the people they think they're competing with in many instances they're not competing with at all even if the value proposition sounds very similar. In this case, health advice for women. So anytime we look at a list we want to have a sense of is it a brand sensitive list? Now if we have a million of them it may not be true in every case but in the aggregate when you're dealing with a million people these kinds of theories actually work very well. So we could say that someone who bought from legs is much more brand sensitive than someone who bought from hosiery corporation of America which nobody has heard of but does about half a million transactions a month based on other aspects of their value proposition like value and convenience and replenishing the supply every month and so forth. So we just want to have a sense of brand sensitivity of the people on the list we're mailing to based on the transaction they engaged in and the organization that they bought from. question then I guess uh from the pile here how and why did you get into copywriting in the first place um good question as I mentioned before I grew up uh in Brooklyn on the other side of the east river and my parents were married during world war two I was born in 1946 after the war ended and during those days there were very few apartments no apartments being built in New York so after the war was over and new families wanted to get started there were very few apartments available so my parents are very happy that they had landed this two-room apartment in crown heights Brooklyn and that's not two bedrooms that's two rooms a kitchen and a bedroom and that's where I grew up in this tiny little two-room apartment and uh all four of us slept in the bedroom didn't seem unusual to me that wasn't unusual what was unusual and to answer the question was because my friends would tell me this this is a strange apartment and it was strange because as small as it was there was all kinds of merchandise around the walls and the reason the reason this was was because my dad was an amateur copywriter during the 30s a great depression when he became you know a man and he was grown he he got a job with the city of New York was a very secure job and he considered himself very lucky to have gotten that job and so that's a job he always wanted to stay with didn't pay much but he always liked to enter these contests now back in those days they didn't have sweepstakes at least not many the way that that companies would get their uh product out there and get people to be excited about them would be right in 25 words or 50 words or less why you like a certain type of product and my dad was brilliant at this he could write
